speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about coming to Landsberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Landsberg, yes. We came to Landsberg. We came to Landsberg in 1944, at the-- the end of it. Because 1945, November, my son was born. And when we came there, there was really, the tragedies just began because people just found out that they don't have anybody. They were looking for relatives. And the different organizations were helping, but they couldn't find, and the tragedy really just began. And-- but life has to go on. People were getting married. Older people were marrying brides that they were younger than their own daughters before the war. But in the years later, there were lots of children. And it was-- you can see a smile already in that-- in that camp. And there was about 5,000 people. And by the end, I was there for four years. There were schools and synagogues, people were-- but that's all we were thinking, is about eating and enjoying ourselves because we knew that that was only temporary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the displaced persons camp open before the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think so. I don't recall exactly, but I'm sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you support yourself while you were in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We supported? We didn't have to support ourselves. The American people were so nice. They were UNRRA, you know? And they supported us. And I was still a teenager and I had my-- my son. And so I used to even get chocolate. Yeah, they-- they supported us [SIGH] completely. People came that they were like skeletons, and then when they left in four years, they all looked good, well, the ones who didn't die.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had health services there, and food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a hospital. I remember the doctor's name, Dr. [? Nabrisky. ?] He was the one who delivered the boy, my son. And they even circumcised him in the hospital, too. They didn't want to trust anybody. My son was the first Jewish baby in the-- in the camp because he was born in '45. I was married in '44. And it-- everybody was just hoping that someday, we're going to leave that-- that country drenched with the Jewish blood. And we used to-- lots of people, we used to go to school. There's [INAUDIBLE] schools there, and and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- the-- the tragedy was only that we couldn't find anybody. Some people found some kind of relatives. And-- and my brother came, my other brother, Chil, the one who is still alive. He came, and he was with us in Germany. I forgot to say that he reunited-- he came to us in Warkowicze. And this is how he got to the Polish Army. Because he went away-- he went away to Ternopil to work, because they didn't have any work. And if they didn't have any work, there wasn't nothing to eat. This was when the Russians were still there. I forgot to-- to talk about it. And in Germany, everybody got one room, but we got two rooms, because my both brothers lived with us. We used to occupy these barracks that the German soldiers used to have. And at the end, there was like 5,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in Landsberg, did you expect to stay a short time? Did you know how long you were going to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We didn't even know if we'd be able to go any place. It just then became, this is where they're supposed to go, the displaced persons camp. And-- and from there, because this is where everybody goes. It came, and I remember when Palestine was declared to Israel, I mean, independent country, that a lots of people were-- were going to Palestine at that time, volunteer. And lots of our survivors didn't live that long to see Israel as our country, because they-- they were killed in the war over there. And the most people who went to Palestine at that time, to Israel, was singles. The families stayed, didn't go over to Israel. With-- if you had a baby, they just were waiting to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family was waiting to go to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also forgot, I also forgot-- I have to go back when I remember. When I talk, I remember things. The-- the reason we went to Poland is, we wanted to take our brother-- my brother Joseph out. He was in the Polish Army. So I went to the Paruchnik, and I told him-- I showed him papers, because I had papers that after-- that I can collect some money, but I needed this brother-- my brother to sign for. So-- so the Paruchnik let him go for one day. He said to me that my face looks very honest. I will not make him desert, to be a deserter, and that's what exactly I did. We took him out from there, from in Bursha, and we went-- he went away with us to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1945 for that. And-- because the Russians-- the Russians were there. And they were looking for him, because he was-- he ran away from the army. Even long afterwards, he was always worried about it. And so-- so we were-- we went to Germany, and my brothers lived with me-- with us. And then we were there until 1949. And June-- I think it was in June. And at-- that we received-- that we can go-- the news that we can go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you need a sponsor to go to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody had to have a sponsor, and we had a sponsor from St. Joseph, Missouri. The United Jewish Appeal, they sponsored us. But we were the only-- we were the only Jewish family in this little town. And I just want to say a little bit about my trip. We went with the ship, and everybody was sick. But I was very fortunate. Somehow, I was a hero everywhere. I wasn't sick. And I was going up and looking at the sunrise, sunset. And I looked at all these waves coming. It was so beautiful. And then one day, it was like a hurricane. We thought that the whole ship is going down. But in a few hours, all this was over, and the ocean was just like glass. And the biggest excitement was when we came, I guess, when we saw the Statue of Liberty in New York. And they took the train to St. Joseph, Missouri. This is where we wound up. It was a small city, and it was very hard to get used to, but the people were very nice. I had a teacher who was teaching me-- teaching English. And we were there for 10 years. I worked in a factory. I went to work. I wanted to buy a car, so I went to work. And my husband worked in the same factory. And social Jewish life was very good there. Jewish life was very good there. We belonged to synagogue, and I belonged to the Sisterhood, and [INAUDIBLE]. And it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later on, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we went to-- we were going to leave St. Joseph, Missouri to Los Angeles. We went to-- to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we went through a lot in Los Angeles. At first, it was hard, but then we went into business, and everything was going real good. And my husband got sick, and he had cancer. And he died when I was 38 years old, with two sons. But I didn't want these two boys to stay in the store. I wanted them to get education. So my one son went to Berkeley, became a lawyer. And the other one also went to Berkeley. And he is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what does he do? He makes documentaries in Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were at the store, we lived-- I, personally, lived through lots of terrible things, almost as bad as during the war. I had four holdups in one month with a gun. I think that God really wanted me to-- to live because the same day that guy who-- who was holding, standing with me about 15 minutes, with the gun touching my chest, he went down the street, another liquor store, and he killed the wife with the husband. And three times, I had to lay down on the floor. When the first time, when I had my first holdup. He said I should lay down. I stood up, and I said, you know, I didn't want to lay down to be shot by the Germans in my back, and I'm not going to do it now. Because, by the way talking about this, I just remembered an incident that has happened to me. That they were take our family, they want to shoot us. And I turned around, and I said, I don't want to be shot in the back. I want to be shot in-- that I look at you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- that was that was even before we left Zwolen. That was in Poland. It was the first day, because somebody told them that we have a [NON-ENGLISH]. What is a [NON-ENGLISH]? A [INAUDIBLE]. But they couldn't find it, because we throwed it in-- we found out it's not good to have it, so they throwed it in-- in Poland, you know they had the toilets outside? So this is where we throwed it in. I just-- that's why I mentioned it about-- I mentioned it about that I didn't want to be shot in the back, I want to be shot in front.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gave him the money. Going back to the holdups, I gave him the money, and he just said that it wasn't enough. I said, that's all I have because my son just went there and went to the bank. And so they-- they just left. I was lucky. I had lots of friends that were killed with holdups.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went through a lot. I still was in the business for five-- for five years for myself with all the holdups, until I got rid of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess, come-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from all the things, I got so nervous, I think, talking I didn't even mention that I had another son. And my son Theodore was born in St. Joseph, Missouri. On July 15, we were blessed with another son, Theodore. It's kind of hard to talk about all this. I didn't realize how it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have people, or has your family asked you about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, yes. In fact, I had made videos for my children and grandchildren. I have-- while I was-- while I was studying English, I was writing my life story. And then I recorded this on a little recorders, and I gave this to the grandchildren, even when they were little. They weren't even able to understand anything. And they're really very interested. They always ask me to-- to-- record this. And my father-- my father's wish is, like, coming true. Because that's all he wanted me is to tell the story, to live it through. That was his words. That's how I-- That's how he said it. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add, anything that I didn't ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: I keep forgetting. You know, when you get this age, and 50 years later, it's amazing. Important things, forget. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I-- I only hope that this will never happen again. And it should go through the generations, that they should know what has happened in our generation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think that all the volunteers who really take their time to do this interviews, I think, it's wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my family. My mother, my father, my sister, three brothers, and me. On the first row to the left is my mother, Hinda. Her maiden name is Freedman-Greenberg. Next is my little brother, Walwale Greenberg. My father-- next is my father, Towia Greenberg, and me, Brindel Greenberg. To the left, on second row, is my brother, Chil Greenberg, and Dwoire Greenberg, and Joseph Greenberg. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This photo was made in Poland, in the beginning of 1939. This is my sister, Gina Greenberg. This photo was taken in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Year, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] around 1939, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1939. This photo is the automobiles that my father had a company and in the middle is standing my cousin, Meyer Tenenbaum, which he is deceased now. He used to be the chauffeur over there. And this was taken in Zwolen in Poland, around 1938. This picture is my wedding picture. My first husband, Zelek Weisburg. It was taken in Zdolbunow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland, in 1944. These are my two sons. To the left is Theodore Weisburg and to the right is Jacob Weisburg. This picture was taken in Los Angeles. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] about two years ago, in '93. This is the family of my oldest son, Jacob. To the left is his oldest daughter, Leah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to the right is his youngest daughter, Deborah. And next to my son is his wife [? Moricah. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was taken in Fresno, California, in 1994. This is Liv, the oldest daughter of my son, Teddy, Theodore. Liv Weisburg, and this photo was taken in Stockholm, Sweden in 1995. Forgot. I have to wait. This is the youngest daughter of my son, Theodore, Rebecca Weisburg. Was taken in Stockholm, Sweden in 1994. This is my wedding picture of my beloved husband, Roman Greenbaum, which I married 23 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His name is Roman Greenbaum, and this was taken in Los Angeles [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1972.
